content.text: Why Interview Methods Change for AI-Capability Discovery

Standard usability interviews ask whether a designed interface meets user needs. AI-capability discovery interviews ask something harder: which tasks in a person's work could AI reliably assist, at what level of autonomy, and under what trust conditions. The distinction matters because participants cannot evaluate a capability they have never seen, and product teams cannot spec a system whose operational edge cases they do not yet know. Traditional requirements interviews anchor on existing workflows; AI discovery interviews must surface latent work, hidden constraints, and the mental models that will govern adoption.

This topic covers a structured four-phase interview protocol, transcript coding methods, and the artifacts that bridge user research to engineering requirements. The examples draw on clinical documentation AI, where the stakes of misplaced trust are high enough that the failure modes are unusually visible — which makes it a useful teaching domain even for teams building in other sectors.
The Four-Phase Interview Framework

A single 75-minute session moves through four phases, each serving a different research function. Running them in order prevents confirmation bias: you learn what participants actually do before showing them what AI might do.

attrs.alt: Four-phase AI capability discovery interview framework showing Current State, Mental Models, Prototype Reaction, and Scope Negotiation phases with their output artifacts
attrs.title: Four-phase AI capability discovery interview framework showing Current State, Mental Models, Prototype Reaction, and Scope Negotiation phases with their output artifacts

content.text: Phase 1 — Current State Mapping (20 min): Document the workflow as it exists. Identify where time is lost, where errors occur, and who is involved.
Phase 2 — AI Expectations and Mental Models (15 min): Surface assumptions about what AI does and should do — before any prototype is shown.
Phase 3 — Prototype Reaction and Boundary Discovery (25 min): Present a thin prototype or Wizard-of-Oz simulation. Observe interaction and collect edge cases.
Phase 4 — Scope and Success Negotiation (15 min): Convert findings into a prioritised capability list and a first definition of success.
Interview Guide: Full YAML Specification

Store interview guides in version control alongside code. This makes it possible to correlate finding batches with guide versions, replay protocols for follow-up studies, and hand guides to third-party research vendors without information loss.

# ai-capability-discovery-interview-guide.yaml
meta:
  project: "Clinical Documentation AI"
  version: "2.1"
  duration_minutes: 75
  interviewer_notes: "Record with consent. Probe silences — they often reveal hidden constraints."

phases:
  - id: current_state
    label: "Phase 1 — Current State Mapping"
    duration_minutes: 20
    goal: "Understand the actual workflow before proposing AI involvement"
    questions:
      - text: "Walk me through the last time you completed [target task] end to end."
        probe: "What information did you need that wasn't immediately available?"
      - text: "Where do you spend time you wish you didn't have to?"
        probe: "What would you do with that recovered time?"
      - text: "When something goes wrong with [task], what does that look like?"
        probe: "How do you find out? How do you fix it?"
      - text: "Who else touches this work before or after you?"
        probe: "Are there handoffs that slow things down?"
    artifacts_to_collect:
      - Current SOP documents or checklists
      - Example inputs and outputs (sanitized)
      - Error logs or incident reports if available

  - id: ai_expectations
    label: "Phase 2 — AI Mental Models and Expectations"
    duration_minutes: 15
    goal: "Surface assumptions and fears before showing anything"
    questions:
      - text: "Have you used AI tools at work or personally? Tell me about that."
        probe: "What worked? What frustrated you?"
      - text: "If I told you AI would help with [task], what would you hope it does?"
        probe: "What would make you trust it enough to use it daily?"
      - text: "What would have to go wrong for you to stop using it entirely?"
        probe: "Has something like that happened with other tools?"
      - text: "Who in your organization decides whether a new AI tool is acceptable?"
        probe: "What criteria do they use?"

  - id: prototype_reaction
    label: "Phase 3 — Prototype Reaction and Boundary Discovery"
    duration_minutes: 25
    goal: "Observe how participants interact with a working prototype"
    setup: "Show a Wizard-of-Oz or thin prototype performing the core capability"
    tasks:
      - instruction: "Please use this as if I weren't here. Think out loud."
        observe: ["First click", "Hesitation points", "Attempts to verify output"]
      - instruction: "Now try to break it — give it something you'd be worried about."
        observe: ["Edge cases surfaced", "Adversarial inputs", "Trust boundary violations"]
    debrief_questions:
      - "What surprised you — good or bad?"
      - "What would you change before using this with a real [customer/patient/order]?"
      - "Is there anything this should never do, even if it could?"

  - id: scope_negotiation
    label: "Phase 4 — Scope and Success Negotiation"
    duration_minutes: 15
    goal: "Convert findings into prioritised capability requirements"
    questions:
      - text: "If this only did one thing perfectly, what should that be?"
        probe: "Why that one over the others we discussed?"
      - text: "What does 'good enough' look like for your first month of use?"
        probe: "How would you know it crossed that threshold?"
      - text: "What would you tell a colleague to convince them to try this?"
        probe: "What objections would they raise? How would you answer?"

analysis:
  coding_framework:
    capability_requests: "Features mentioned ≥ 3 times across participants"
    trust_thresholds: "Conditions named as prerequisites for adoption"
    edge_cases: "Inputs or scenarios participants warned would cause failures"
    anti_goals: "Explicit statements of what the AI must not do"
  synthesis_outputs:
    - "Prioritised capability list with participant frequency counts"
    - "Trust requirement matrix by user role"
    - "Edge case catalog with likelihood and severity ratings"
    - "Scope negotiation brief for the first sprint"
Phase 1 — Current State Mapping
Why Workflow-First Matters

AI product teams routinely over-specify the wrong capability because they start from a solution hypothesis rather than the task structure. Current-state mapping replaces 'would you like AI to do X?' with 'walk me through your last real instance of X.' The distinction is not minor: participants answering hypothetical questions are limited by their imagination of what AI does; participants narrating recent events surface the actual friction, data sources, and error recovery steps that define a meaningful scope.

The goal of this phase is not a general process description but a task model accurate enough to drive data-flow requirements. Pay particular attention to information that participants gather from sources that are not in the primary system — a spreadsheet someone maintains personally, a verbal handoff from a colleague, an email they search when the formal record is incomplete. These informal data sources are both the richest targets for AI and the hardest to make available to a model.
Ask about the last real instance of the task, not a hypothetical or general description
Probe every data source: where does the information come from? Is it always available? Is it always accurate?
Ask what participants do when the system is unavailable — the workarounds reveal the minimum viable path
Document the handoff points: who receives the output and what do they do with it?
Note where participants check their own work — these are the natural positions for AI verification steps
Observation Techniques

If the workflow involves a system, ask participants to share their screen and perform the task live while narrating. Live observation surfaces micro-decisions that participants would not think to mention in a verbal account — a double-check against a printed reference, a workaround for a missing field, a confirmation step with a colleague. These micro-decisions often represent the highest-value AI integration points because they happen at scale and are invisible to system logs.
Phase 2 — AI Expectations and Mental Models
The Trust Baseline Before the Prototype

This phase must precede any prototype or demo. Showing AI output before calibrating mental models contaminates the research: participants' stated expectations and concerns become anchored to the specific implementation rather than the task domain. You want to understand what trust level participants would require before the prototype shapes what they think is possible.

Three mental model dimensions are particularly predictive of adoption and requirement quality. First, the autonomy boundary: at what decision point does a participant expect to remain in control? Second, the error tolerance: how many mistakes per unit of output is acceptable, and what class of mistake is categorically unacceptable? Third, the accountability attribution: who is responsible when AI output causes a downstream error — the user who accepted it, the team that deployed it, or the vendor?
Autonomy boundary: 'What should the AI never do without your explicit approval?'
Error tolerance: 'If the AI got this wrong once out of twenty times, would you still use it?'
Accountability: 'If a colleague made a mistake using AI output, who would be responsible?'
Prior AI experience: ask about consumer tools too — ChatGPT experience shapes enterprise expectations
Influencer mapping: 'Who else would have to agree for this to be adopted in your team?'
Extracting Implicit Trust Conditions

Participants rarely state trust conditions in formal terms. They surface as hedges ('as long as I can verify it'), as conditions ('I'd use it if it always showed me where it got the information'), and as fears ('what happens when it gets it wrong and no one catches it'). Train interviewers to recognise these linguistic patterns and probe them into explicit statements. A trust condition is the most actionable output of this phase because it translates directly into a non-functional requirement: 'source attribution on every output' or 'human confirmation required before any write action.'
Phase 3 — Prototype Reaction and Boundary Discovery
Prototype Fidelity for Discovery Research

The prototype for Phase 3 should be the minimum fidelity needed to generate a genuine reaction. For most AI capabilities, a Wizard-of-Oz approach works well: the interviewer or a colleague manually produces the AI output in real time while the participant believes a model is running. This approach lets you test the experience of receiving AI output without building a functional model, which means you can run discovery research weeks before engineering starts.

If a thin functional prototype exists, use it — but constrain the interaction to the specific capability being evaluated. Avoid general-purpose demos that let participants explore unrelated features; they consume time and generate noise in the data.
Wizard-of-Oz: interviewer produces output manually while participant interacts with a mock UI
Clickable mockup: static screens with realistic AI output for a specific task
Thin functional prototype: a real model call behind a minimal interface
Avoid polished demos — production-quality UI cues make participants reluctant to criticise
Always include a 'try to break it' task — it surfaces edge cases faster than any question
Capturing Edge Cases in Real Time

When a participant says 'what if the patient has two allergies that conflict?' or 'what about when the previous note was from a different department?', that is an edge case. Capture it verbatim. Edge cases from user research are almost always operationally real — they come from lived experience, not imagination. A single session with eight to twelve participants will surface edge cases that engineering and QA teams would not discover until production.

Code each edge case immediately after the session with three fields: description, example input or scenario, and severity. Severity is determined by the consequence of the AI handling the edge case incorrectly — not by how frequently it might occur. A low-frequency blocker (patient misidentification) ranks above a high-frequency medium issue (slightly awkward phrasing in a generated summary).
Phase 4 — Scope and Success Negotiation
Converting Research to Prioritised Requirements

The final phase uses the previous three phases' findings to negotiate scope. The key question — 'if this only did one thing perfectly, what should that be?' — is deliberately forcing. Participants who have watched a prototype and articulated their trust conditions are well-positioned to answer it. Their answer is the north-star capability for the first sprint.

The phrase 'negotiation' is deliberate: this is not a requirements-gathering exercise where the interviewer passively records what participants want. The interviewer brings the constraint — engineering capacity, model capability limits, data availability — and uses the conversation to find the highest-value scope that is also buildable. Participants who understand constraints make better scope decisions than those who are asked to wish-list.
Present one realistic constraint: 'We can do this reliably 80% of the time or do half of it reliably 99% of the time — which do you prefer?'
Ask for a success definition that participants can observe: 'What would you see on your screen that would tell you it is working?'
Ask what would cause them to stop using it: this becomes the guardrail metric
Confirm the anti-goals explicitly: 'Is there anything we agreed it must never do?'
End with: 'What would you tell a colleague to get them to try this?' — it reveals the value proposition in participant language
Transcript Coding and Synthesis
Coding Framework

Transcript coding converts verbatim session records into structured data that can be aggregated across participants. Consistent codes allow frequency analysis (which capability was requested by 9 out of 12 participants?), trust matrix construction (what conditions did compliance officers name that physicians did not?), and edge-case prioritisation. Without a coding scheme, synthesis requires rereading all transcripts for every question, which does not scale beyond four or five sessions.
Code Category
Definition
Example Verbatim
Output Artifact
capability_request
A task the participant wants AI to perform or assist with
'It would be great if it just drafted the note from the encounter'
Prioritised capability list
trust_condition
A prerequisite the participant names for adopting the capability
'Only if I can see what it used to generate that'
Trust requirement matrix
edge_case
A scenario the participant warns will cause incorrect AI output
'What about patients with the same name in the same ward?'
Edge case catalog
anti_goal
An action the participant says the AI must never take
'It should never send anything without my sign-off'
Anti-goal register
success_indicator
An observable signal the participant would use to judge success
'I'd know it's working if my documentation time drops below ten minutes'
Success metric candidates
blocker_concern
A risk the participant says would prevent adoption entirely
'If the compliance team hasn't approved the PHI handling, no one will touch it'
Adoption risk register
Synthesis Script: Python Implementation

After coding sessions, aggregation is mechanical. This script consumes a directory of coded transcript JSON files and produces a structured ResearchArtifacts object and a scope negotiation brief suitable for handoff to engineering.
Real-World Example: Clinical Documentation AI
Study Setup

A health system considering AI-assisted progress note drafting ran twelve discovery interviews across four roles: physicians (3), clinical coordinators (4), nurses (3), and compliance officers (2). Sessions followed the four-phase protocol. All participants were given a Wizard-of-Oz prototype: the interviewer typed a simulated AI draft in real time after the participant narrated a recent encounter, then shared the screen.

The synthesis revealed a significant gap between what the product team expected to build and what participants needed. The team had planned to generate complete discharge summaries; participants prioritised progress note drafting for daily encounters. The discharge summary was a lower-frequency, higher-stakes document for which compliance requirements would have added months to the timeline.
Scope Negotiation Brief: YAML Output

The scope brief is the primary handoff artifact from user research to product and engineering. Version it alongside the interview guide so future teams can trace which brief corresponded to which study round.

# scope-negotiation-brief.yaml
# Generated from 12 clinical-documentation AI interviews
# Participants: 4 clinical coordinators, 3 physicians, 3 nurses, 2 compliance officers

prioritised_capabilities:
  - capability: draft_progress_note
    participant_count: 11
    trust_conditions:
      - "Physician must review before any note is saved to chart"
      - "Source sections must be traceable to specific encounter data"
    sprint_target: 1

  - capability: flag_abnormal_values
    participant_count: 9
    trust_conditions:
      - "Threshold logic must match hospital policy document, version controlled"
      - "Alert fatigue guardrail: max 3 flags per note"
    sprint_target: 1

  - capability: route_to_specialist
    participant_count: 7
    trust_conditions:
      - "Routing suggestion only; physician confirms before message sent"
      - "Must respect on-call schedule integration"
    sprint_target: 2

  - capability: generate_discharge_summary
    participant_count: 5
    trust_conditions:
      - "Must pull from structured fields, not free-text interpretation"
      - "Compliance officer sign-off on template required"
    sprint_target: 3

trust_matrix:
  physician:
    - "AI output is a draft — I'm always the author of record"
    - "I need to know what it used to make its suggestion"
    - "Errors must be catchable before they leave my screen"
  clinical_coordinator:
    - "If it creates more clicks than it saves, I won't use it"
    - "It should know our specific order sets and protocols"
  nurse:
    - "Alerts need to go to the right person, not just whoever is logged in"
    - "I need an easy way to mark something as 'not applicable'"
  compliance_officer:
    - "Every AI-assisted field must be flagged in the audit trail"
    - "PHI handling must be documented and approved before pilot"

edge_cases:
  - severity: blocker
    description: "Patient with multiple allergies and conflicting medications"
    example: "Warfarin + aspirin + new NSAID prescription"
    mitigation: "Must defer to pharmacist check; never generate note without allergy reconciliation"

  - severity: high
    description: "Encounter data from previous visit loaded instead of current"
    example: "Patient seen twice in one day; AI uses morning note for afternoon summary"
    mitigation: "Encounter ID pinning; explicit timestamp display"

  - severity: high
    description: "Specialist note not yet signed appears in summary"
    example: "Pending cardiology consult treated as confirmed diagnosis"
    mitigation: "Only use signed, co-signed records; pending status clearly labelled"

  - severity: medium
    description: "Non-standard abbreviations in physician free-text"
    example: "c/o SOB w/ exertion → AI fails to expand correctly"
    mitigation: "Abbreviation dictionary per department; flag unknowns for human review"

anti_goals:
  - "AI must never submit, send, or save any document without explicit physician confirmation"
  - "AI must never infer diagnosis from symptoms — diagnosis codes must come from physician"
  - "AI must never display raw model confidence scores to clinical users"
  - "AI must never access records outside the current patient's encounter without explicit navigation"
  - "AI must never summarise using only the most recent note when a longitudinal view is required"

success_definition:
  north_star: "A physician spends < 2 minutes reviewing and approving an AI-drafted progress note"
  guardrail_metrics:
    - metric: "Undetected AI errors reaching signed notes"
      threshold: "0 per quarter"
    - metric: "User override rate on AI routing suggestions"
      threshold: "< 30% (higher = AI not learning domain)"
    - metric: "Time-to-first-use after deployment"
      threshold: "< 5 working days for 80% of target users"
Common Interview Failures and How to Avoid Them
Leading with the Solution

The most common failure in AI discovery research is describing the planned AI capability before completing Phase 1. This contaminates the current-state data: participants anchor their workflow descriptions to the solution you described rather than their actual practice. If participants ask about the AI before Phase 3, say: 'I want to understand how you work first — I'll show you what we're thinking about in about twenty minutes.'
Conflating Interest with Commitment

'That would be useful' does not mean 'I would use that.' Participants are polite and hypothetical interest is easy to express. Probe every positive response with: 'How does that compare to how you do it now? Would you change your current approach for that?' The honest answer often reveals that the workflow change required to adopt the AI is itself a barrier.
Interest probe: 'How often would you actually use that feature in a typical week?'
Adoption probe: 'What would you have to stop doing to make room for this in your workflow?'
Trust probe: 'What would you do differently if the AI got this wrong 5% of the time?'
Influencer probe: 'Whose approval would you need before adding a new tool to your workflow?'
Edge case probe: 'What's the most unusual [patient/order/case] you've dealt with recently?'
Recruiting the Wrong Participants

AI discovery research requires participants who perform the target task frequently enough that they have strong opinions about its edge cases. A physician who documents twice a week has fewer edge cases in memory than one who documents thirty times a week. Screen for task frequency, not just job title. Aim for at least one participant who has experienced a significant error in the target workflow — they will surface the highest-severity edge cases and the most articulate trust conditions.
From Interviews to Functional Requirements

The artifacts from discovery interviews map directly to requirement types. Capability requests with frequency counts above a threshold become user story candidates. Trust conditions become non-functional requirements in the constraint and safety categories. Edge cases become acceptance criteria and QA test cases. Anti-goals become hard constraints in the system prompt and guardrail logic. The scope negotiation brief defines the first sprint's scope and the success metrics the team will track.
Research Artifact
Requirement Type
Where It Goes
Capability request (≥3 participants)
User story / feature scope
Product backlog, prioritised by frequency
Trust condition
Non-functional requirement (safety, transparency)
NFR register, system prompt constraints
Edge case (blocker/high)
Acceptance criterion
Definition of Done, QA test cases
Anti-goal
Hard constraint
System prompt, guardrail evaluation suite
Success indicator
Outcome metric
Dashboard, sprint review criteria
Blocker concern
Adoption risk
Risk register, stakeholder communication plan
Run at least 8 sessions before synthesis — frequency patterns stabilise around 6-8 participants for common capabilities
Involve engineering in at least two sessions as silent observers — it accelerates feasibility triage
Share the scope negotiation brief with participants before finalising — it validates synthesis accuracy
Re-run Phase 3 after the first working prototype — first-round mental models shift with real model output
Treat anti-goals as immutable constraints during design; revisit only with full participant group consent
Key Takeaways
Sequence matters: map current state before showing AI, and surface mental models before showing any prototype. Skipping either step contaminates the data.
Trust conditions are requirements: every 'as long as' or 'only if' from a participant is a non-functional requirement in disguise. Code and track them explicitly.
Edge cases come from lived experience: participant edge cases are almost always real operational scenarios that QA would not discover until production.
Anti-goals define the hard boundary: scope the first sprint around what AI must do; anti-goals define what it must never do, and those constraints drive system prompt design and evaluation criteria.

The scope brief is the handoff artifact: a versioned, structured synthesis of capability priorities, trust conditions, edge cases, and anti-goals gives engineering a clear starting point and makes the research replayable.
